coredata.dc:description: In recent decades, analytical techniques have increasingly focused on the precise quantification. Achieving this goal has been accomplished with conventional analytical approaches that typically require extensive pretreatment methods, significant reagent usage, and expensive instruments. The need for rapid, simple, and highly selective identification platforms has become increasingly pronounced. Molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) has emerged as a promising avenue for developing advanced sensors that can potentially surpass the limitations of conventional detection methods. In recent years, the application of MIP-silica materials-based sensors has garnered significant attention owing to their distinctive characteristics. These types of probes hold a distinct advantage in their remarkable stability and durability, all of which provide a suitable sensing platform in severe environments. Moreover, the substrate composed of silica materials offers a vast surface area for binding, thereby facilitating the efficient detection of even minuscule concentrations of targets. As a result, sensors based on MIP-silica materials have the potential to be widely applied in various industries, including medical diagnosis, and food safety. In the present review, we have conducted an in-depth analysis of the latest research developments in the field of MIPs-silica materials based sensors, with a focus on succinctly summarizing and elucidating the most crucial findings. This is the first comprehensive review of integration MIPs with silica materials in electrochemical (EC) and optical probes for biomedical analysis and food safety.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In recent decades, analytical techniques have increasingly focused on the precise quantification. Achieving this goal has been accomplished with conventional analytical approaches that typically require extensive pretreatment methods, significant reagent usage, and expensive instruments. The need for rapid, simple, and highly selective identification platforms has become increasingly pronounced. Molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) has emerged as a promising avenue for developing advanced sensors that can potentially surpass the limitations of conventional detection methods. In recent years, the application of MIP-silica materials-based sensors has garnered significant attention owing to their distinctive characteristics. These types of probes hold a distinct advantage in their remarkable stability and durability, all of which provide a suitable sensing platform in severe environments. Moreover, the substrate composed of silica materials offers a vast surface area for binding, thereby facilitating the efficient detection of even minuscule concentrations of targets. As a result, sensors based on MIP-silica materials have the potential to be widely applied in various industries, including medical diagnosis, and food safety. In the present review, we have conducted an in-depth analysis of the latest research developments in the field of MIPs-silica materials based sensors, with a focus on succinctly summarizing and elucidating the most crucial findings. This is the first comprehensive review of integration MIPs with silica materials in electrochemical (EC) and optical probes for biomedical analysis and food safety.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Accurate identification of analytes encompasses biomolecules, heavy metal ions, volatile organic compounds, and others, can play an important role in enhancing human life expectancy through disease identification, treatment, and food safety [1]. Consequently, researchers have directed their efforts toward the development of cost-effective sensors that exhibit exceptional sensitivity, low detection limits, long-term durability, reproducibility, and specificity [2,3]. A sensor is an analytical device that converts the presence of a physical or chemical species into a measurable signal, either quantitatively or semi-quantitatively [4,5]. In other words, the main function of the sensor consists of two basic steps: recognition and transmission [6]. During the recognition process, the analyte engages in interactions with the receptor molecules or active sites present within the structure of the sensor's recognition element [7]. Simultaneously, the transducer component of the sensor converts this molecular event into a valuable and measurable signal, such as resistance, current, or potential [8–10]. During the last decade, various biomaterials and materials have been approved to meet the increasing demand for analytical sensors in various fields such as biomedical analysis and food safety [11,12]. Among various (bio)materials are the synthetic polymers capable to imitate biological systems, namely MIPs which until now have found vast application in the separation science.

MIPs are synthetic materials, which draw inspiration from biological systems, exhibiting the ability to selectively bind to target molecules [13]. In other words, MIPs are frequently employed as artificial antibody and antigen systems, functioning through a mechanism akin to the 'lock and key' principle observed in biological systems [14,15]. These polymers can be synthesized via the copolymerization of functional monomers and cross-linkers, all of which facilitated by the presence of target molecules that serves as molecular template [16]. The primary function of the monomer is introduction functional groups capable of engaging in covalent or non-covalent interactions with the template, thereby facilitating the formation of the adduct [17]. Following copolymerization, the template molecules are eliminated from the polymer matrix through chemical decomposition or solvent extraction methods [18]. Consequently, the highly cross-linked polymer matrix develops recognition cavities that show shape, size, and chemical functionality complementary to the template molecule [19]. These cavities enable selective rebinding of the template molecules from intricate mixtures of closely related compounds. MIPs demonstrate remarkable structural predictability, sensitivity and discerning identification capabilities [20]. In detail, the predictability of the structure of MIPs, as a crucial aspect of their design and application, refers to the ability to anticipate and control the arrangement of functional monomers around the template molecule, resulting in a complementary binding cavity upon template removal. The proliferation of comprehensive and commendable reviews pertaining to the subject of MIPs signifies the accelerated advancement of contemporary trends and areas within the field in recent years [21,22]. On the other hand, in practice, the preparation of MIPs faces the challenge of sustainable approaches. Recently, the detection of environmental risks associated with the unsustainability of MIPs (i.e., creation, precursors, use, end-of-life, and recycling) is considered green MIPs [23]. The following one or more greenification principles present more far-reaching applications compared to their counterparts. Among different principles, stimuli-responsive/self-cleaning MIPs, optimization with a computational model, enhanced operator safety, controlling polymerization without energy waste, increment degradability of MIPs in the environmental condition, elimination template by green solvent, and recovery of waste can be supposed most important principles [24,25]. For instance, in order to cleaning or regenerate MIPs, the stimuli-responsive/self-cleaning is frequently achieved through the exploitation of stimuli-responsive materials or mechanisms, such as the application of heat, light, or changes in pH, disrupting the interactions between the bound molecules and the polymer.

Generally, although MIPs show selectivity, they still have some inherent defects such as lower binding capacity, irregular shape, and difficult removal of the template used in the matrix structure [26–28]. To elaborate, there are specific situations in which they may fail to compete effectively with other recognition elements. For instance, the comparison of the binding affinity of MIPs with enzymes or antibodies reveals that MIPs have lower binding affinities. Sensitivity to environmental conditions can suppose another limitation. In other words, although biological recognition elements have the ability to adapt and evolve over time in response to changing environmental conditions, MIPs lack this ability. Furthermore, due to the importance of the size and shape of the target molecule in designing MIPs, the complexity of structure or undergoing conformational changes can be considered as a barrier to recognize it accurately. Therefore, the combination of MIPs with other materials, as one of the efficient techniques, can improve the performance of MIPs in bio(sensing) [29]. In this regard, the conjugation of MIPs with silica materials present as a propitious strategy in the realm of sensor design and development [30,31]. Silica materials, renowned for their remarkable mechanical, thermal, and chemical stability, bestow a robust support matrix for MIPs, thereby augmenting their performance and functionality in bio(sensor) applications [32,33]. The integration of MIPs into silica materials offers significant advantages. Firstly, the inherent porosity of silica materials facilitates the effective immobilization of MIPs, resulting in a substantial loading capacity and enhanced accessibility of binding sites for target analytes and this, in turn, engenders heightened sensitivity and selectivity of sensors [34]. Furthermore, the integration of MIPs with silica materials facilitates the creation of durable probes [35]. Silica materials confer excellent mechanical strength and exhibit resilience against challenging environmental conditions, thereby ensuring the stability of sensors [36]. In addition, using silica materials, it is possible to create surface MIPs that simplify the removal of template molecules by placing template sites on the surface [37]. The combining of MIPs with silica materials can introduce high potential sensors which used in various fields including food safety, biomedical diagnostics and food safety, among others [38]. According to the sensitivity and selectivity of these robust probes, the application of them can consider as versatile solutions for addressing the challenges in these domains. Recently, one of the excellent review articles published in this field not only emphasized the role of the MIPs process in the formation of highly specific siloxane-based nanomaterials, but also compared the performance of MIPs based on siloxanes and other organic polymers. On the other hand, there is a shortage of discussion on the integration of other types of functional materials with silica-based MIPs for the construction of analytical sensors for food safety and biomedical analysis [39]. Among different techniques, surface modification of silica and incorporation of nanomaterials can be considered important methods for introducing hybrid probes. To illustrate, the compatibility of silica-based MIPs can be improved with surface modification, which involve treatments such as attachment of specific functional groups. These modifications can lead to increased selectivity and sensitivity, making them more suitable for use in complex matrices. Therefore, surface modification of silica-based MIPs is a crucial step toward the development of highly efficient and reliable detection systems for food safety and biomedical analysis. Furthermore, the incorporation of different nanomaterials with silica-based MIPs can improve their properties such as sensitivity and selectivity [40,41].

Over the last few years, the detailed investigation reveals that due to the importance and development of several biosensors based on MIPs, there have been many review articles that investigated the role of MIPs in the fabrication of efficient analytical methods [42–45]. For example, Ayerdurai et al. [44], reviewed the application of MIPs in presenting high performance EC biosensors. In another paper, Yang and co-workers [45], highlighted the role of MIPs in optical fiber-based biosensors. In another review article, Sobiech and colleagues [43], studied the conjugation of carbon-based materials with MIPs. Consequently, this review aims to comprehensively investigate the performance of bio(sensors) utilizing MIP-silica materials as bio(sensing) probes for monitoring food safety and biomedical analytes. MIPs and silica materials have emerged as highly promising probes for attaining the aforementioned objective due to numerous notable advantages. To provide further clarification, this review study will categorize into employment methodologies for measuring biomedical analysis and food safety. Additionally, the main methods discussed in this review were EC, and optical bio(sensors).

ce:section.ce:section-title: MIP-silica materials-based sensors in food safety
ce:section.ce:para: The escalating requirement of the food supply has emerged as a paramount global health concern, necessitating a heightened focus on food safety. The presence of a range of food contaminants, such as chemical substances, environmental pollutants, and residual materials causes different issues within the realm of food safety [46–48]. These contaminants exhibit a high degree of resistance to degradation and have a propensity for bioaccumulation within the human body, thereby posing significant risks to human health [49,50]. There is a critical need to develop sensors that are characterized by simplicity, sensitivity, and cost-effectiveness for monitoring and quantifying food samples [51]. In this section, we will review MIP-silica material-based EC and optical sensors that have been developed to detect food analytes.

EC analysis emerges as a viable and cost-effective technique for the detection and quantification of electroactive species within a given solution [52,53]. This technique offers the advantage of enabling both qualitative and quantitative analysis, making it a straightforward and user-friendly method [54]. The occurrence of oxidation-reduction reactions is the basic principle underlying the operation of EC biosensors, which takes place between the sensor regions and the analyte molecules [55]. The incorporation of polymers in silica-based materials can improve the performance of bio(sensors). Indeed, interaction capabilities and biocompatibility of sensors are significantly increased by using polymers in the structure of MIP-based silica materials [56]. For example, dopamine with catechol and amine groups can be self-polymerized in weakly alkaline media. In this regard, Bonyadi et al. [57], designed an EC sensor based on molecular imprinted polydopamine (PDA)-coated silica nanoparticles for the quantitative analysis of sunset yellow (SY) (Fig. 1 ). In detail, in the presence of SY, dopamine was self-polymerized to synthesis PDA imprinted materials which coated with silicon dioxide (SiO2) nanoparticles layer, resulting in the formation of SiO2@MIP PDA nanoparticles on surface of the carbon paste electrode (CPE). Under optimal conditions, the differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) method was used to analyze SY in multiple food samples with a limit of detection (LOD) of 1.5 nM and a linear range of 4.5 nM–9.1 μM, respectively.

Tryptophan (Trp), an amino acid, has been attracting considerable attention in sensing platforms, especially in EC sensors. For instance, Kaya and co-workers [58], used a solution comprising l-tryptophan (L-Trp) and tetraethoxysilane (TEOS) for depositing onto a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) through hydrolysis/condensation reaction for capturing of bisphenol F. In the presence of the target, the performance of the developed probe was examined by the DPV method which able to provide a sensitive sensing platform with a LOD of 0.291 fM in the linear range of 1–10 fM. Moreover, the quantification of bisphenol F in spiked bottled water demonstrated a recovery rate ranging from 98.83 % to 101.03 %. Despite the fact that the employment of poly(amino acids) in the structure of MIP/mesoporous silica nanoparticles can bring about high potential analytical approaches, the presence of carbon-based and metallic-based nanomaterial can boost the performance of these sensing platforms significantly [59]. To explain further, carbon-based nanomaterials reveal brilliant properties such as high surface area, excellent electrical conductivity, high chemical stability and good biocompatibility, all of which has led to an increasing interest in EC sensors for food safety purpose [60]. Among different carbon-based nanomaterials, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs), carbon dots (CDs), and quantum dots (QDs) have attracted considerable interest [61]. For instance, Huang and colleagues [62], developed a highly sensitive EC assay by incorporating MWCNTs into molecularly imprinted silica nanospheres which was employed on the surface of EC electrode for detection of chlorpyrifos. The prepared platform had a linear relationship between the EC response and the target analyte concentration in the range of 5 pM–50 nM. Additionally, the engineered platform revealed acceptable sensitivity in the quantitative analysis of chlorpyrifos in fruits and vegetables. In another similar example, a synthesis of vinyl silica materials-coated MWCNTs in an alkaline environment was used to 2,4-dichlorophenoxyacetic acid herbicide detection in milk [63]. For this purpose, MIPs were prepared on the surface of the MWCNTs (through a free radical polymerization process) which dissolved in a chitosan solution and deposited onto a GCE. Under normal circumstances, voltammetric technique was applied to investigate the performance of GCE modified by MWCNTs-SiO2-MIPs for the detection the target. The reported approach was able to detect 2,4-dichlorophenoxyacetic acid with a LOD of 4.273 nM and a linear range of 0.01–100 μM. In addition, this assay demonstrated recovery rates ranging from 96.2 % to 102.4 % and exhibiting a relative standard deviation (RSD) of less than 4.74 %.

The use of metal nanomaterials on the probes based on MIPs and silica materials can be considered another efficient strategy for improving performance sensing due to several advantages such as high catalytic activity, increased surface area, tunable physicochemical properties, long-term stability, and importantly, compatibility with different electrode substrates [64]. For example, Somnet et al. [65], constructed an EC sensor utilizing a screen-printed graphene paste electrode (SPGrE) for paraquat determination. This study represented the pioneering use of paraquat-imprinted polymers synthesized on the surface of platinum nanoparticles (PtNPs), which drop-casted onto the surface of the SPGrE, resulting in the fabrication of a highly efficient sensor (PtNPs@MIP/SPGrE). This platform was successfully employed for the accurate determination of paraquat residues in vegetables and fruits.

Considering the many advantages of poly(amino acids), carbon-based nanomaterials and metal-based nanomaterials, their use alongside MIPs and silica materials is very effective in obtaining good analytical results. Therefore, it is expected that a wide range of these nanomaterials will be used in EC sensors developed to food safety (see Table 1).

In recent times, optical sensors have emerged as an exceedingly valuable analytical instrument, functioning by monitoring alterations in the intensity of light beams or variations in their phases when interacting with physical systems [66]. Optical assays exhibit notable advantages in comparison to conventional sensors consisting sensitivity, cost-effectiveness and specificity [67]. The objective of this section is to investigate the utilization of optical techniques employing MIP-silica materials for the identification and quantification of food safety.

The integration of mesoporous silica nanomaterials and MIPs offers a promising approach for the development of optical probes. The mesoporous nature of silica materials endows it with a substantial surface area, enabling enhanced interaction with analytes [68]. In parallel, MIPs contribute highly specific binding sites that possess the ability to selectively capture target analytes [69]. The synergistic combination of these facilitates efficient analyte recognition, leading effective signal transduction. As an illustration, Wang and co-workers [70], developed a novel fluorescent sensor using encapsulation SiO2 nanoparticles with a MIP which contained allyl fluorescein for τ-fluvalinate in vodka. This core-shell fluorescent molecular imprinted probe was successfully synthesized by using methyl methacrylate as the functional monomer, high fluorine cypermethirin as the template molecules, and allyl fluorescein as the optical materials. In detail, there was a good linear relationship between the fluorescence (FL) signal of the prepared probe (MIPs-dye@SiO2) and the concentration of the target in the linear range of 0–150 nM. Therefore, the quantitative detection of τ-fluvalinate with a LOD of 13.251 nM was achieved by exploiting this probe.

GQDs, as graphene sheets smaller than 100 nm, have emerged as crucial components in the advancement of optical probes owing to their distinctive properties including strong fluorescent activity, minimal toxicity, robust chemical inertness, and high water solubility [71]. This type of carbon-based nanomaterial demonstrates excellent optical characteristics including elevated light absorption, proficient FL emission, and remarkable photostability [72]. For instance, Zhou et al. [73], designed a fluorescent probe based on integration of silica-coated GQD and MIPs to identify 4-nitrophenol (4-NP). In this protocol, a straightforward hydrothermal approach, tetraethoxysilane and 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTS) were employed to synthesize silica materials-coated GQDs, cross-linker and functional monomer, respectively (Fig. 2 ). The mechanism of FL quenching phenomenon of the MIP-coated GQDs composite can be attributed to the resonance energy transfer from GQDs (donor) to 4-NP (acceptor). This energy transfer process efficiently suppresses the FL emission of the composite when 4-NP molecules rebind to the specific binding sites. Remarkably, the composite demonstrated an excellent linear range (0.02–3 μg mL−1), exhibiting a good correlation between the analyte concentration and the FL quenching response.

Although rapid and specific detection ability of QDs in complex samples attracted considerable attention, they can easily be affected by signal fluctuations and background interference during quantification. In addition, the large diameter of QDs (2–3 nm in diameter) can restrict anchor of them inside the MIP pores. reduction of the internal analyte mass transfer efficiency can be considered another negative consequences of integration QDs with MIPs. Hence, some scholars use multilevel mesoporous silica in the structure of probes which contained both small (2–3 nm) and large (>5 nm) mesopores. The small mesopores can increase the internal analyte mass transfer efficiency, and alongside that, the large pores present sufficient space for the anchoring of QDs. For instance, Wei and colleagues [80], developed a ratiometric fluorescent sensor utilizing molecularly imprinted multilevel mesoporous silica (MIFP-SiCQDs@cadmium telluride (CdTe) QDs) which was specifically designed for the highly sensitive detection of imidacloprid. In this study, a post-imprinting modification technique was employed to anchor CdTe QDs onto the multilevel mesoporous structure of MIFP-SiCQDs for enhancing analytical signal. In the reported sensing approach, in the imidacloprid range of 5 ng mL−1 to 0.5 μg mL−1, the photoluminescence intensity of the CdTe QDs was gradually decreased, while the FL of SiCQDs remained relatively constant.

The utilization of magnetic nanomaterials in the structure of silica-based MIPs can be considered another strategy for improving performance of optical biosensors. Indeed, the excellent catalysis activity of these nanomaterials can increase output signal and this, in turn, improve the sensitivity of optical biosensors [81]. Furthermore, modification routes and low toxicity, and larger surface areas to volume ratio are other advantages of these nanomaterials [82]. As an example, Li and colleagues [83], successfully developed a novel fluorescent nanosensor using a molecularly imprinted silica layer appended to iron selenide (FeSe) QDs (MIP-FeSe-QDs) for the determination of cyfluthrin (CYF) (Fig. 6 ). In detail, the FL quenching of FeSe-QDs in the developed probe can be considered as principle of this sensing approach. In this protocol, a strong linear correlation was observed between the FL signal and the increasing concentration of the target in the range of 0.01–0.2 mg L−1. The efficiency of the developed probe for the quantification of CYF in real samples including fish was demonstrated successful recoveries ranging from 88.0 to 113.9 %, with RSD below 6.8 %.

One of the important challenges of MIPs-based silica materials in optical sensors is the lack of sensitivity. The review of recent studies demonstrates that the use of hybrid nanomaterials can be considered one of the advanced strategies to overcome this problem. Another important challenge is a limited dynamic range that implements different optical signal amplification methods including FL resonance energy transfer (FRET) and Surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) can extend the dynamic range of these analytical approaches. Furthermore, in a few cases, MIPs based on silica materials reveal cross-reactivity with structurally similar molecules and this, in turn, causes false-positive results. In this regard, optimizing the imprinting process and predicting binding sites can be supposed important solution for this problem. Therefore, it is expected that the increasing number of using a wide range of metal nanomaterials and carbon nanomaterials with MIPs-based silica to create novel optical sensors in the future [84,85]. The investigation into the shortage of these probes in food safety reveals that they can be specifically designed for detecting microorganisms (such as bacteria and viruses) and toxins, especially mycotoxins, in the future (see Table 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: MIP-silica materials-based sensors in biomedical analytes detection
ce:section.ce:para: Biomedical analytes encompass substances or molecules found within biological samples serving as crucial indicators of an organism's physiological or pathological condition [86]. These analytes consist a wide range of components including enzymes, biomarkers, drugs, hormones, metabolites, and disease-associated molecules [87]. Significant attention has been paid by scholars in recent years to the detection of biomedical analytes, which holds great importance in the realms of biomedical research, clinical diagnostics, and personalized medicine [88]. Precise and prompt detection of these analytes plays a pivotal role in disease diagnosis, monitoring treatment effectiveness, and early identification of potential health hazards [89]. EC and optical sensors have emerged as highly advantageous tools for the detection of biomedical analytes, both of which offering a multitude of benefits. Optical and EC assays suggest non-invasive or minimally invasive techniques for detecting analytes in biological specimens such as blood, urine, or tissue samples [90]. In this part of the review, we will deal with the optical and EC based on MIP-silica materials sensors developed for the identification of biomedical analytes and drugs including lamivudine, zidovudine, alanine, NE, ephedrine, ketamine, ascorbic acid, metronidazole, phenobarbital, tetracycline, and bovine hemoglobin.

The biochemical reaction between the target and recognition element produces a measurable EC signal which typically based on changes in the electrical properties of the system. The EC signals can contain changes in voltage, current, and impedance [91]. In order to improve the sensitivity and selectivity of EC bio(sensors), researchers have made great progress by incorporating diverse nanomaterials and bioreceptors in the structure of bio(sensors). Interestingly, Aryan et al. [92], developed an EC sensor based on nanostructured MIP for the quantitative analysis of alanine in urine samples. For this purpose, the sol-gel surface imprinting technique was employed to coat the MIP layer onto silica nanoparticles, resulting in the formation of MIP@SiO2. By exploiting the DPV technique, the performance of the modification EC electrode with SiO2@MIP was evaluated for alanine detection and it was demonstrated a LOD of 0.6 μM in the linear range of 10–500 μM. The performance of the developed sensor was effectively assessed for the quantification of alanine in urine samples, yielding favorable outcomes.

Importantly, excellent surface-to-volume ratios, large surface area, and effective diffusion rate of core-shell MIP can arouse interest in development these types of sensors [93]. Elaborately, hollow porous MIPs, as one of the common structures of core-shell structure, introduce efficient recognition sites of MIPs owing to the presence of recognition site from the inner and outer double surfaces. In these structures, sacrificial material is necessary which can be provided by silica materials due to chemical stability and easy removal [94]. For instance, Prasad and colleagues [95], introduced a novel and straightforward approach for synthesizing of MIP-based core-shells, hollow porous MIPs, which used on the sensing zone of a pencil graphite electrode (PGE) in terms of sensitive detection of zidovudine and lamivudine in real samples. In this study, hollow core-shell structure was synthesized by exploiting imprinted polymers on the surface of vinylated silica nanospheres. In detail, upon removal of the core, the hollow structure provided a greater number of selective cavities in the polymer structure through both the inner and outer interface of the polymeric layer for interaction with the targets. The obtained LOD of reported sensing assay for lamivudine and zidovudine in aqueous samples were found to be as low as 2.23 and 1.26 ng mL−1, respectively. In blood serum, the LOD were 2.45 and 1.88 ng mL−1 for lamivudine and zidovudine, respectively. In 2016, Bagheri and colleagues [96], employed another core-shell nano-structured based on Fe3O4@SiO2@TiO2-MIPs for ephedrine determination. Indeed, the presence of metal oxides (TiO2) and magnetic nanomaterials (Fe3O4) in the structure of core-shell presents specific properties such as high catalytic activity which improve the performance of the sensing approach. Under normal conditions, the performance of the designed sensor was evaluated by the DPV technique which showed a LOD of 0.0036 mM and a linear range of 0.009–2.8 mM. Due to the importance of core-shell structure in probes based on MIP-based silica materials, a similar sensing platform was implemented on the sensing zone of screen-printed electrode for point-of-care (POC) analysis of illicit drugs [97]. For this purpose, the surface of screen-printed electrode was decorated with polyalanine to improve the potential signal stability. Subsequently, the immobilization of MIP-based silica materials was conducted for capturing ketamine hydrochloride with a detection limit of 9.29 × 10−7 M.

Among the alternative nano-cores, palladium nanoparticles (PdNPs) are one of the excellent choices owing to their conductivity, extraordinary catalytic, and EC features. For example, a new composite of MIP-coated Pd nanoparticles was synthesized via the sol-gel method for norepinephrine (NE) quantification (Fig. 7 ) [98]. In this work, the integration of Pd nanoparticles with a silica materials-based MIP exhibited favorable EC catalytic properties, a significant surface area, and exceptional selectivity. Under optimal circumstances, the performance of engineered approach was evaluated by the DPV technique. The LOD obtained by this sensor was 0.1 μM, which indicated the high performance of the sensing platform. In addition, this approach was able to identify the target molecule in urine and injection samples.

Generally, great progress has been made in the development of spherical MIPs which can boost biomedical analysis. To illustrate, there is a massive shortage in using various structures such as multi core in one shell, single hole hollow, homogenous mesoporous, mesoporous core solid shell, and solid care mesoporous shell in EC sensors for biomedical analysis. In view of hollow MIPs, introducing high-potential hollow MIPs-based silica materials can be achieved by paying attention to some factors during the application and synthesis. Among them, hollow structure dimensions can be considered one of the important matters that can influence mass transport, binding capacity, and overall performance. Furthermore, monomer selection is another important research direction in this field to determine selectivity and affinity. In addition, the role of polymerization temperature and time in presenting high-performance MIP-based silica materials is undeniable.

Another functional monomer used in the formation of complex MIPs-based silica materials with various templates is methacrylic acid. To illustrate, this functional monomer demonstrates excellent advantages including flexibility, durability, and robustness. Importantly, brilliant binding efficacy with a wide range of targets can be supposed another important advantages of this functional monomer. However, the application of methacrylic acid as a monomer in the synthesis of MIPs is the propensity of methacrylic acid to undergo facile dimerization, which can contribute to the heterogeneity of the resulting MIPs [108]. Thanks to the numerous ways of interaction such as H-bond receptor, H-bond donor, dipole-dipole interaction, and van der Waals interactions, methacrylic acid can interact strongly with templates. In this regard, Zhao and colleagues [109], employed a silanization technique to enhance the fluorescent properties of CDs for the detection of bovine hemoglobin. In this protocol, the surface of CDs coated with SiO2 for improving FL performance. The presence of methacrylic acid in the structure of CDs@SiO2@MIP introduced remarkable selectivity probe for bovine hemoglobin detection with a linear range of 0.31–1.55 μM, and a LOD of 1.55 μM.

The developed sensors still have shortcomings in favor of slow response and limited chemical stability, which lead to low lifetime and sensor rate. To elaborate, the performance of optical bio(sensors) based on MIPs-silica nanomaterials is influenced by several variables. Indeed, controlling them is pivotal to achieving desired sensing properties. Among them, the characteristics of the silica nanomaterials, the composition and structure of the MIPs, the binding kinetics between the analyte and the MIPs, the environmental conditions, and the detection instrumentation can consider as important variables. Significantly, one of them is choosing a monomer which determines the selectivity and affinity of the MIPs. For this, a review of different aspects and applications of functional monomers can be considered efficient strategies in biomedical analytes detection. Another is the concentration of cross-linkers influencing the rigidity, porosity, and stability of the MIPs. Polymerization time and temperature can be considered other important factors in introducing high potential MIPs [110]. In terms of target, there is a massive shortage in the application of MIP-silica materials in detection of glucose, DNA, RNA, specific proteins and hormones for the diagnosis of different diseases and cancers (see Table 2 ).

In conclusion, this article provides a comprehensive overview of the latest advancements in the field of silica materials-based MIP for sensitive and selective food contamination and biomedical analytes detection. The incorporation of MIP and silica materials as a sensing element introduces a synergistic combination of the unique properties of MIP and silica materials, making it a promising candidate for use in optical and EC assays. Silica materials could be a good support matrix for MIP due to their interesting physicochemical stability. However, the fabricated morphologies are unsatisfactory. For instance, the dispersion of spherical MIPs on the surface of silica materials is unsatisfactory. In detail, agglomeration and adhesion are often observed in many core-shell MIPs, making it difficult to apply the recognition sites due to the thick shell. Hollow MIPs are prone to collapse and fracture easily and imprinting in the pores of mesoporous silica still poses a challenge. Furthermore, despite several developments in field of MIPs-based bio(sensors), numerous hollow MIPs collapse fracture and easily, and this, of course, make imprinting in the pores of mesoporous silica difficult. The analysis of MIP-silica materials-based assays for food safety and biomedical analytes demonstrates that optical sensors have received more attention than EC sensors. In order to enhance the selectivity and sensitivity of MIP-silica materials-based sensors, diverse nanomaterials were used in the structure of MIP-based silica materials. To elaborate, the combination of different nanomaterials, silica nanomaterials, and MIP lead to the emergence of nanohybrids that have a good analytical performance in detecting different target. Interestingly, the siloxane-based conjugates that possess MIP-layer composed from naturally derived different macromolecules such as chitosan can be considered efficient probes for future works. On the other hand, there is shortage in application of novel nanostructures such as MXene, Xenes, antibody, and aptamer. In order to maximize the efficacy of the obtained results, it is highly recommended to employ MIP-silica materials in conjunction with novel nanomaterials. Despite the significant potential of MIP-silica materials in the analysis of food contamination and biomedical compounds, there are still several limitations and research gaps that need to be improved in future studies. In order to achieve the objective of cost-effective, user-friendly, and automated detection of biomedical and food contamination analytes, we propose the utilization of smartphone sensors and portable microchips based on MIP-silica materials technology. Elaborately, the ubiquitous presence of smartphones can make them powerful and versatile tool in biosensors, especially MIP-based silica materials. Their digital imaging capabilities, coupled with built-in sensors, render smartphones an ideal platform for portable and rapid quantification. In particular, the integration of smartphones with MIP-based biosensors is anticipated to open new avenues of research due to several advantages including portability, connectivity, accessibility, and affordability. The confluence of these technologies is expected to facilitate a range of applications, from disease diagnosis to food safety. Furthermore, we propose the integration of cutting-edge nanomaterials, specifically layered double hydroxide (LDH), and metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), in combination with MIP-silica materials, as a highly promising strategy for the development of microchips with the ability to detect multiple analytes simultaneously. Finally, we highly offer the design and implementation of MIP-silica materials smartphone sensors and microchips capable of conducting simultaneous quantitative analysis of various biomedical and food contamination analytes.

Xu Yan: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Investigation, Conceptualization. Yasir Qasim Almajidi: Writing – review & editing, Investigation.
